"I think we need people in Congress that are ready to stand up, stand up for the American people, and here in Nevada, stand up for Nevadans."

Flippo spent much of his military career in the Middle East. He argues that Trump's Iran policy is ending a war, not starting one:

"What President Trump is doing with the Strait of Hormuz and Iran, this has been a 47-year conflict. Some in the media have been saying that Trump is starting a forever war, but in my opinion, he's ending it. 

"Most all of my career was in the Middle East, fighting because of Iran and what they're doing with Israel and with Hezbollah and Hamas and the Houthis. What Iran has done is over, and things are going to change, and President Trump sees that.

"I fully support what President Trump is doing. We need to open up the strait, it needs to be a clear waterway. We need the flow of oil to start again."

The voters of northern Nevada want a constitutional conservative, Flippo argues, and he is prepared to deliver results on budgetary issues, as the exploding national debt approaches $40 trillion.

"We have to get to a balanced budget. We have to get this deficit under control. What are we, $39 trillion in debt now and it keeps climbing? Something has to be done. We have to stop spending money that we don't have. 

"We need to stop expanding government. And that's what's been happening over the years. We just keep expanding and expanding. You know, I'm a constitutional conservative: government has four purposes and if you go beyond those four purposes, it belongs to the states. 

"And the government has taken on so much now that we just keep getting more and more in debt. And it's time that we have representatives in Congress that are ready to stop that."

Illegal immigration is a signature issue for Flippo, who paints the problem in economic terms, and is concerned about its impact on a variety of pressing public policy issues.

"lllegal immigration is a problem in the United States. And if you look at what's happened in the last six years under Biden and the open borders policies and the 20 to 30 million people that were allowed to come in, what has that done? It's caused housing costs to go up. 

NEVADA LT GOV: ELON MUSK COULD MAKE SPACE TOURISM STATE'S NEXT BIG INDUSTRY

"That's a supply and demand issue. 20 million people. That's 20 cities the size of Las Vegas, right? What does that mean? That means all those schools, the police departments, the fire departments, all those social service programs. All that has to be paid for by tax dollars, by taxpayers. 20 cities the size of Las Vegas, 40 cities the size of Reno. That's a lot. That's a lot to ask for the taxpayers to come in all at once."

While the district leans Republican, the Democrats believe they have fielded a formidable candidate in former Nevada Assembly Majority Leader Teresa Benitez-Thompson. Flippo is not taking anything for granted, but believes the contrast between his conservative outlook and her left-wing voting record will play to his advantage.

"She's a big government [supporter], big spender. She's voted to raise taxes 12 times here in Nevada. She voted for the largest tax increase in Nevada's history. She voted for Planned Parenthood funding. She voted for drivers licenses for illegals. I mean, she has a voting record that she says she's very proud of. And I don't believe that's something to be proud of."

Finally, Flippo believes that Trump's support is a net asset in his district, and believes that support, along with the backing of Gov. Lombardo, will carry a lot of weight.

"My district in northern Nevada, District 2, is an R+7 that Trump won by 14. So Trump obviously carries some weight in northern Nevada. It's a conservative part of this state, which is different than the southern part. 

"Getting on a team with the governor being united, getting the vote out is going to be critical. And having Trump on my side up in the north is going to be a huge factor in winning this."

item.title: Hunter Biden reveals he never wanted Joe Biden to run for re-election, but refused to tell him to stop
item.description: Hunter Biden told NPR he never wanted his father to seek re-election, saying the presidency makes it hard for anyone to be there for their own family.
item.content:encoded:
Hunter Biden revealed in an interview on Saturday that he never wanted his father, former President Joe Biden, to run for a second term.

Over the past few months, Hunter has been on a podcast tour speaking out about what it was like to be Biden’s son during his presidency and offering his take on politics. Following the full federal pardon granted by his father in December 2024, Hunter has made use of his freedom to speak off-the-cuff. 

During the recent interview with NPR, Hunter was asked by the host of NPR’s "Newsmakers" whether he wanted his father to have run for re-election in the first place.

"No. No," Hunter replied.

EX-BIDEN STAFFER BLAMES BIDEN'S 'HUBRIS' FOR THE 'HELLSCAPE THAT WE LIVE IN': REPORT

When asked whether he had told then-President Joe Biden this, Hunter answered, "No. I told him this, is that I would like I'd done my entire life, is that I supported whatever he thought was best as it related to what he wanted to do to serve."

"There was nothing that was more important to me than getting my dad back, getting my family back," he added further. "And the presidency does something that people I don't think fully comprehend. A president — it's almost impossible for a president to be fully present in the lives of the people that he loves the most, because it is literally all-consuming. It is an all-consuming thing."

Hunter rejected reports that he had egged his dad on to stay in the running, replying, "Literally nothing could be further from the truth, and I think that they know that."

He then turned the question around on the media and asked, "But just from a logical perspective, what would I possibly have to gain to continue to be the target that I was?"

JILL BIDEN SAYS FORMER PRESIDENT WILL LIVE WITH STAGE 4 CANCER 'FOR THE REST OF HIS LIFE,' HAS SLOWED DOWN

"The four years of his presidency were, I think, by anybody's estimation, a really difficult four years — a lot of it my own making, but a lot of it just simply because of who he was and the position that he held — in which the struggles that I was trying to overcome in sobriety, sober this entire time, building a family and rebuilding a family, to do it while he was president was maybe one of the most difficult things that I can ever imagine putting someone through," he said.

"And more than anything, I just wanted to have my dad back," he concluded.

The NPR host then asked whether he let his father know about these concerns before he decided to run for re-election. Hunter replied that he only aired these concerns after Joe had made his choice, arguing that otherwise it would have been unfair to burden his father with his own concerns.

"No, because I think it's unfair for me to do because this — because I know this also. There's only one thing my dad cares about more than his service, and that's me and my mom, my sister, and his grandkids, my children," he said. "There's only one thing that he cared about more than his job, and that's his family. And for somebody in his family, particularly me, to say, 'Dad, please don't do it,' I don't know how — I think that's incredibly unfair to do to somebody. I would never want to be that. I would never want it to be the reason that he didn't fulfill what he believed was his duty and service."

CLICK HERE FOR MORE COVERAGE OF MEDIA AND CULTURE

item.title: Wholesome exchange between popular cosplayer and WWE Superstar Stephanie Vaquer at Fanatics Fest went viral
item.description: Chilean cosplayer Fabibi World Cosplay met WWE's Stephanie Vaquer at Fanatics Fest wearing a handmade tribute costume that went viral on social media.
item.content:encoded:
There was more to this weekend's Fanatics Fest than Tom Brady's continued bizarre back and forth with Logan Paul. The slap and the rumors of the best quarterback who ever lived climbing in a wrestling ring filled up some timelines.

Others had more wholesome content for them to consume. If you were one of the lucky ones, in addition to the Brady-Paul nonsense, you had a Chilean cosplayer and a Chilean professional wrestler meeting one another.

Popular cosplayer Fabiola Morales, known as Fabibi World Cosplay, showed up to the event dressed as WWE Superstar Stephanie Vaquer. The two shared a special moment and the cosplayer even left with an autograph.

ZERO BS. JUST DAKICH. TAKE THE DON’T @ ME PODCAST ON THE ROAD. DOWNLOAD NOW!

"It was an honor to pay tribute to my fellow Chilean, meet her in person, and receive her autograph on my cosplay," Fabibi World Cosplay wrote after meeting Vaquer.

"Thank you, Stephanie, for being so kind to every fan and for proudly representing Chile on the world’s biggest stage. So proud of you, La Primera."

LIV MORGAN RETURNS TO THE TOP OF WOMEN’S DIVISION WITH WRESTLEMANIA 42 WIN OVER STEPHANIE VAQUER

The exchange between the two and much of the content surrounding the cosplayer paying homage to the wrestler went viral on social media. Especially the "100% handmade costume" that was a "100% faithful design to the original."

You don't have to go out of your way with a phony beef to get noticed, as a couple of Chilean ladies who enjoy the intricacies of a well-designed costume can attest to. A touching meeting will do.

Put your all into something and the rewards follow closely behind. That's the message being delivered here with a simple tribute to a pro wrestler.

The effort was acknowledged with a hug and an autograph directly on the costume. It doesn't get much better than that at one of these things.

There was, I think we can all agree, some magic that took place when Fabiola Morales and Stephanie Vaquer met in New York City this weekend.

I'll take them over Tom Brady and Logan Paul any day.

item.title: New York Times torched for ‘FALSE’ reporting on ICE investigations, outlet fires back
item.description: DHS accused The New York Times of false reporting about FBI no longer investigating confrontations with ICE officers, sparking a public clash.
item.content:encoded:
The Department of Homeland Security and The New York Times are going toe-to-toe after the agency accused the outlet of "false" reporting alleging that federal agents across the country were told that the FBI would not be investigating confrontations with ICE officers.

"This is FALSE," DHS posted on social media. The agency said that "the relationship between DHS and [the Justice Department] in investigating assault on federal officer cases has NOT changed, and [the FBI] will continue to investigate in accordance with agency policy."

"This administration has been clear, anyone who assaults law enforcement will be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law," DHS added.

In response, Danielle Rhoades Ha, a spokesperson for The New York Times, said the outlet stands by its reporting, telling Fox News Digital, "We're confident in the accuracy of our reporting, which revealed that Federal agents around the country were told in recent days that the F.B.I. would no longer investigate confrontations with immigration agents."

TRUMP BLASTS NEW YORK TIMES, CNN FOR 'SEDITIOUS' COVERAGE OF IRAN WAR

Rhoades Ha pointed out that the story "included a joint statement from the Justice Department and Department of Homeland Security denying that such a change would be enacted."

The outlet reported that federal agents around the country were told in recent days that the FBI would no longer investigate confrontations with immigration agents, cases that The New York Times wrote, "Sometimes yield evidence that could be used to prosecute Department of Homeland Security agents implicated in violent encounters."

The Times wrote that if such a policy were enacted it would "sharply limit law enforcement scrutiny of immigration agents" and be "effectively leaving the agency to investigate itself."

FBI DIRECTOR KASH PATEL, NEW YORK TIMES CLASH OVER PAPER'S CLAIM ITS REPORTER WAS 'INVESTIGATED'

The outlet added that this comes as the Trump administration "draws mounting criticism over killings at the hands of federal officers."

ICE officers were involved in two fatal shootings this month in Maine and Texas. This has led to renewed protests against immigration enforcement operations. DHS, meanwhile, has called attention to significantly increased attacks on federal law enforcement officers in the line of duty.

In response to The New York Times, DHS and DOJ doubled down that the report is categorically false and that the relationship between the two agencies has not changed.

HARRIS CALLS FOR ICE PROBE AFTER MAINE SHOOTING AMID RENEWED 'BORDER CZAR' CRITICISM

In a joint DHS-DOJ statement shared with Fox News Digital, the agencies reaffirmed they are "partners in addressing crime and upholding the rule of law, while securing the safety and security of American communities."

item.title: Obama warns Vance is pushing 'blood and soil' version of 'We the People' mantra
item.description: Former President Barack Obama accused Vice President JD Vance of pushing a "blood and soil" vision of America despite being married to the daughter of an immigrant.
item.content:encoded:
Former President Barack Obama suggested Thursday that Vice President JD Vance was pushing a "blood and soil" vision for the country despite being married to the "daughter of an immigrant."

The vice president came up as Obama was discussing the Reconstruction era with author Malcolm Gladwell, claiming that the issues from that era are still being discussed today.

"It’s impossible to spend a lot of time thinking about Reconstruction without reflecting on the present day, right?" Obama said. "Because these issues are — well, they’re still there. If somebody is asking why it is relevant to talk about Reconstruction, I have to describe for them the degree to which we’re having the same arguments. The issues of: How are we going to help the freed people who’ve been enslaved? What are we going to give them to allow them to have a go at life? What does real freedom mean?"

'GROSS' SLATE PIECE COMES UNDER FIRE FOR SUGGESTING USHA VANCE PREGNANCY IS POLITICAL STRATEGY

He argued that more directly, they continue to debate over voting rights after the Supreme Court ruling on race-based congressional gerrymandering as well as Vance's political party pushing for a "certain kind of people."

"We have a politics now that at least one of our major parties has been captured by politics that is not that subtle about suggesting that 'We the people' means a certain kind of people," Obama said. "When you have the vice president, current vice president, making a speech that is basically a blood and soil version of 'We the people,' that it matters who your parents were and how long they've been here despite him being married to a daughter of an immigrant himself." 

He continued, "That echoes, then, ideas about who can be a citizen, who belongs, who gets to make decisions. So all these issues are still being debated, sometimes not as bluntly and explicitly as they have in the past, but they're still there."

OBAMA CLAIMS HE OCCUPIES 'A SUITE' IN TRUMP'S MIND, QUESTIONS THE PRESIDENT'S FOCUS ON AMERICANS

"Blood and soil" refers to a phrase popularized by Nazi Germany and associated with White nationalism.

Gladwell went on to accuse Vance of "hypocrisy" for attempting to make a "nativist argument" while being married to Usha Vance, the daughter of Indian immigrants. Obama agreed that Vance was a hypocrite but conceded that it was progress compared to a century prior.

"That is progress. Listen, hypocrisy is progress. It is, because it means that you feel guilty enough to either lie to yourself or others. And that is better than not even thinking about the idea that maybe you’re doing something wrong," Obama said.

Fox News Digital reached out to Vance's office for comment.

Vance's most recent speech was earlier this month honoring America's 250th birthday on July 4 in New York. During his remarks, he urged Americans to reject the "two-dimensional view" of the country based on its failings.

'OBAMA BRO' PODCASTER RAILS AGAINST JD VANCE'S CHANCES IN 2028, CLAIMS 'NO ONE LIKES HIM!'

"So what I'd ask you to do, my fellow Americans, on our 250th birthday, is to reject the two-dimensional view of your fellow citizens and reject the two-dimensional view of your country. Reject that America is a place for zero-sum thinking because it is not. Our history is one of people carving a great civilization out of the wilderness. Reject the view of your nation that sees only its sins, but not its grace and its greatness. Everything that we have done, everything that we've done as a country, we have all done together, not as citizens divided against each other, but as a common people working towards a common future," Vance said.

Obama has largely avoided discussing Vance directly in public comments. He previously took a subtle swipe against Vance during the 2024 election after then-Vice President Kamala Harris chose Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as a running mate.

"When a presidential candidate chooses a running mate, it says a lot about who they are and what kind of president they'll be. Do they pick someone inexperienced and polarizing who will deepen our divisions?" Obama asked. "Or do they pick someone with the judgment to make tough decisions, and the character to believe that every voice counts and everyone deserves an equal chance?"
